reasoning: The vulnerable sink is inside Connection::display() which opens with `! current_user_can( 'manage_options' )` and calls wp_die() for any non-admin visitor. This is a hard WordPress capability gate — no nonce is involved, no token needs to be guessed; the attacker simply cannot reach the page at all without the manage_options capability (i.e., Administrator or Network Admin role). Beyond that hard gate, the injection vector also requires a co-installed plugin or theme to hook the `omnisend_signup_wp_link` filter and return a URL whose naked domain resolves to 'omnisend.com' but that contains a literal double-quote character. A stock WordPress install with only the Omnisend plugin active has no such hook, so the default filtered value is the hardcoded private static $signup_url which contains no special characters. An unauthenticated or low-privilege (Subscriber, Contributor, Editor) attacker cannot reach the admin menu page that calls display(), cannot add hooks at request time, and has no path to supply a crafted URL to the filter. The XSS would only execute in the browser of an already-authenticated Administrator — and only if a malicious or supply-chain-compromised co-plugin also hooks the filter. These combined prerequisites place this firmly in the security-misconfiguration / defence-in-depth category rather than a default-exploitable vulnerability. Severity is downgraded from MEDIUM to LOW.
